blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: accepted as the Giver of Wealth, as in the following translation of a couplet :

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: ‘ If the husband of Kamala begins to give you with his thousands of hands (innumerable), how much can you take with the only two you possess ?’

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: There are temples dedicated to Lakshmi-Nārāyan, and none dedicated to Lakshmi alone, except as Mahā Lakshmi, or the Great Goddess of Wealth, where she is enthroned and attended by two white elephants who are shown pouring water over her head or holding lotus flowers. She is shown squatting, and not with her right leg resting on the lotus used as a foot-stool. In Bombay, white elephants take the place of the owls of Bengal.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: The Bengal Lakshmi is worshipped generally by the dame or priest of a house on a Thursday of the bright half of the months of Pausha (January), Chaitra (March) and Bhādra (August) of every year, and specially on the full-moon day of the month of Aswin (September), on the new-moon day of Kārtik (October). It is believed that she should not be worshipped on any other days. Her worship should be solemnized in the evening and not in the afternoon, nor at night.—[Skandhpurān.]

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: It may be noted that in the Bombay Presidency the worship of Lakshmi takes place on a Friday at twilight, while in Bengal it takes place on a Thursday.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: An up-to-date Bengal tradition of Lakshmi runs :—Once upon a time Lakshmi sent her son Kubera to her daughter Bhushna to instruct her in her own history. Kubera asked her to listen to the story of Lakshmi, but she replied that she could not do so, as by mistake she had chewed betel leaf that morning. Another day Kubera went and asked Bhushna to listen to the story, but on that day she said that she had herself cooked rice for her children and as she was hungry she had partaken of it on that morning.
